en.extract:
The Luzon scops owl (Otus longicornis) or the Luzon highland scops owl is a species of scops owl endemic to Luzon, Philippines. Not to be confused with the Philippine scops owl (Otus megalotis), sometimes referred to as the Luzon lowland scops owl, which is a more common species that shares the same range. The Luzon scops owl, however, is smaller and inhabits higher altitudes than its lowland relative.


Description

The Luzon scops owl is relatively small even among species of scops owls endemic to the Philippines. It has a body length of 18 to 19 cm with a wing length of 13.6 to 15.3 cm. Their large eyes have bright yellow irises, and their beaks are small and slender. Their tarsi are feathered, but only until about halfway through its length and they are equipped with long, sharp claws, typical of birds of prey. Characteristic of scops owls, this species has long ear tufts and an overall dark brown and rufous plumage. Horizontal dark bars irregularly streak across most of its feathers. Areas along its breast, collar and facial disc are speckled white, giving its face the appearance of having white eyebrows. In contrast, the dark brown or rufous feathers on its throat, head and ear tufts are tipped black.
There is little information on this species' body mass, but a select few specimens weighed between 82 and 93 grams.


Taxonomy
The Luzon scops owl is monotypic. Individuals of this species were previously thought to be part of Otus spilocephalus or O. scops, but proper classification by Joe T. Marshall in 1978 based on morphological features and vocalizations eventually earned it the species name Otus longicornis. It is closest genetically to the Mindanao scops owl (O. mirus) and the Mindoro scops owl (O. mindorensis) within the Otus genus, supported by their similar use of high altitude habitats. Besides their habitat preference, a genetic study also supports the evolutionary separation of the highland subgroup of Philippine-endemic scops owl species from those that prefer lowland habitats like the Philippine scops owl and its subspecies (O. megalotis, O. everetti, O. nigrorum) as well as the giant scops owl (O. gurneyi).


Habitat and Distribution
The Luzon scops owl is known to be found in rainforest, montane forest, and tropical pine forest habitats at altitudes generally ranging anywhere from 350 to 1,800 m, but it may also be found at heights upwards of 2,200m.
Although its habitat range across Luzon appears to be relatively large, it is noted to be uncommon and has a patchy distribution that corresponds to its preferred, highland habitats. It has been recorded primarily in northern Luzon provinces such as Ilocos Norte, Benguet, Cagayan, Isabela, Nueva Ecija, or near Manila in Bulacan and Quezon. However, they may be found as far south as provinces like Camarines Norte and Camarines Sur.


Behaviour
According to limited sightings, the Luzon scops owl appears to be a sedentary species, remaining in the same habitat year round.  Like many owls, they have a nocturnal lifestyle.


Vocalizations
Recorded male vocalizations appear to be more frequent at night and consist of a single note that lowers in pitch, each whistle being separated by 3 to 5 seconds of silence. Their vocalization is said to resemble that of the Eurasian scops owl (Otus scops), however the single whistle type of vocalization is characteristic of many other scops owl species.


Diet
Little information is known about the species' diet. However, an inspection of the stomach contents of a few individuals revealed that they may be invertivores, primarily feeding on insects.


Reproduction
Information about reproduction is limited to a few sightings. Notably, chicks were observed to be born in May with light grey down feathers. The owls observed nested in a tree hole with a clutch size of 2 to 3 eggs per nest. Adult Luzon scops owls are thought to begin breeding at one year old with one clutch being produced per year.


Conservation
The IUCN Red List previously assessed the Luzon scops owl to be a near threatened species but in 2023, this was revised to Least-concern species. This downlisting does not mean the populations have been increasing but reflects new data that found this species to be much more common than previously thought. There is no known information on population size and little known on population trends besides the high likelihood that it is decreasing. This species is still declining due to habitat loss. Activities that contribute to deforestation such as logging, mining and the growth of agricultural land in the region of Luzon pose a threat to its endemic species.
It is heavily suggested that critical habitats for the Luzon scops owl and other endangered or threatened birds be protected at all altitudes. Certain important areas Mount Pulag National Park are already protecting highland-dependant species and the Philippine government is working on creating more protected areas through the National Integrated Protected Area System (NIPAS).
This species is found in protected areas including Mount Pulag Protected Park and Mounts Banahaw–San Cristobal Protected Landscape but actual protection from habitat loss and hunting are lax. 
Population surveys and frequent updates on the rates and effects of habitat loss within its range are strongly recommended to give a more accurate assessment of this species' conservation status.


References


External links

Luzon scops owl at OwlPages.com
BirdLife species factsheet for Otus longicornis
"Otus longicornis". Avibase. 
"Luzon scops owl media". Internet Bird Collection.
Luzon scops owl photo gallery at VIREO (Drexel University)
Interactive range map of Otus longicornis at IUCN Red List
Audio recordings of Luzon scops owl on Xeno-canto.
Top audio recordings of Luzon scops owl on eBird.

The '''Luzon scops owl''' ('''''Otus longicornis''''') or the '''Luzon highland scops owl''' is a species of [[scops owl]] endemic to [[Luzon]], [[Philippines]]. Not to be confused with the [[Philippine scops owl]] (''Otus megalotis''), sometimes referred to as the Luzon lowland scops owl, which is a more common species that shares the same range. The Luzon scops owl, however, is smaller and inhabits higher altitudes than its [[Upland and lowland|lowland]] relative.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Sanchez |first=Michael S. |date=2013-03-13 |title=Relative Abundance of the Philippine Scops-Owl Otus megalotis megalotis (Walden) in Marinduque and Mt. Makiling, Philippines |journal=Our Nature |volume=10 |issue=1 |pages=96–114 |doi=10.3126/on.v10i1.7768 |issn=2091-2781|doi-access=free }}</ref>

== Description ==
[[File:Otus longicornis 2009 stamp of the Philippines.jpg|left|thumb|A 2009 Philippine Stamp featuring Luzon Scops Owl]]
The Luzon scops owl is relatively small even among species of scops owls endemic to the Philippines''.'' It has a body length of 18 to 19 cm with a wing length of 13.6 to 15.3 cm. Their large eyes have bright yellow irises, and their beaks are small and slender. Their [[Glossary of bird terms|tarsi]] are feathered, but only until about halfway through its length and they are equipped with long, sharp claws, typical of [[Bird of prey|birds of prey]]. Characteristic of scops owls, this species has long ear tufts and an overall dark brown and rufous plumage. Horizontal dark bars irregularly streak across most of its feathers. Areas along its breast, collar and [[facial disc]] are speckled white, giving its face the appearance of having white eyebrows. In contrast, the dark brown or rufous feathers on its throat, head and ear tufts are tipped black.<ref name=":1">{{Citation |last=Holt |first=Denver W. |title=Luzon Scops-Owl (Otus longicornis) |date=2020-03-04 |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/lusowl1/1.0/introduction |work=Birds of the World |editor-last=Billerman |editor-first=Shawn M. |access-date=2023-10-19 |publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology |language=en |doi=10.2173/bow.lusowl1.01 |last2=Berkley |first2=Regan |last3=Deppe |first3=Caroline |last4=Enríquez |first4=Paula L. |last5=Petersen |first5=Julie L. |last6=Rangel Salazar |first6=José Luis |last7=Segars |first7=Kelley P. |last8=Wood |first8=Kristin L. |last9=Marks |first9=Jeffrey S. |editor2-last=Keeney |editor2-first=Brooke K. |editor3-last=Rodewald |editor3-first=Paul G. |editor4-last=Schulenberg |editor4-first=Thomas S.|url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite journal |last=Marshall |first=J.T. |date=1978 |title=Systematics of Smaller Asian Night Birds Based on Voice |journal=Ornithological Monographs |issue=25 |pages=1–58 |via=Searchable Ornithological Research Archive}}</ref>

There is little information on this species' body mass, but a select few specimens weighed between 82 and 93 grams.<ref name=":3">{{Cite journal |last=Bird |first=Jeremy P. |last2=Martin |first2=Robert |last3=Akçakaya |first3=H. Reşit |last4=Gilroy |first4=James |last5=Burfield |first5=Ian J. |last6=Garnett |first6=Stephen T. |last7=Symes |first7=Andy |last8=Taylor |first8=Joseph |last9=Şekercioğlu |first9=Çağan H. |last10=Butchart |first10=Stuart H. M. |date=2020 |title=Generation lengths of the world's birds and their implications for extinction risk |url=https://conbio.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/cobi.13486 |journal=Conservation Biology |language=en |volume=34 |issue=5 |pages=1252–1261 |doi=10.1111/cobi.13486 |issn=0888-8892}}</ref><ref name=":4">{{Cite web |last=Myhrvold |first=Nathan P. |last2=Baldridge |first2=Elita |last3=Chan |first3=Benjamin |last4=Dhileep Sivam |last5=Freeman |first5=Daniel L. |last6=S. K. Morgan Ernest |date=2016 |title=An amniote life-history database to perform comparative analyses with birds, mammals, and reptiles |url=https://figshare.com/collections/An_amniote_life-history_database_to_perform_comparative_analyses_with_birds_mammals_and_reptiles/3308127/1 |doi=10.6084/M9.FIGSHARE.C.3308127.V1}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Tobias |first=Joseph A. |last2=Sheard |first2=Catherine |last3=Pigot |first3=Alex L. |last4=Devenish |first4=Adam J. M. |last5=Yang |first5=Jingyi |last6=Sayol |first6=Ferran |last7=Neate‐Clegg |first7=Montague H. C. |last8=Alioravainen |first8=Nico |last9=Weeks |first9=Thomas L. |last10=Barber |first10=Robert A. |last11=Walkden |first11=Patrick A. |last12=MacGregor |first12=Hannah E. A. |last13=Jones |first13=Samuel E. I. |last14=Vincent |first14=Claire |last15=Phillips |first15=Anna G. |date=2022 |editor-last=Coulson |editor-first=Tim |title=AVONET: morphological, ecological and geographical data for all birds |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/ele.13898 |journal=Ecology Letters |language=en |volume=25 |issue=3 |pages=581–597 |doi=10.1111/ele.13898 |issn=1461-023X|doi-access=free |hdl=1893/34052 |hdl-access=free }}</ref>

== Taxonomy ==

The Luzon scops owl is [[Monotypic taxon|monotypic]]. Individuals of this species were previously thought to be part of ''[[Mountain scops owl|Otus spilocephalus]]'' or ''[[Eurasian scops owl|O. scops]]'', but proper classification by [https://ornithologyexchange.org/forums/topic/22789-joe-t-marshall-1918-2015/ Joe T. Marshall] in 1978 based on [[Morphology (biology)|morphological features]] and vocalizations eventually earned it the species name ''Otus longicornis''.<ref name=":1" /><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Miranda |first=Hector C. |last2=Brooks |first2=Daniel M. |last3=Kennedy |first3=Robert S. |date=2011 |title=Phylogeny and Taxonomic Review of Philippine Lowland Scops Owls (Strigiformes): Parallel Diversification of Highland and Lowland Clades |url=http://www.bioone.org/doi/abs/10.1676/10-185.1 |journal=The Wilson Journal of Ornithology |language=en |volume=123 |issue=3 |pages=441–453 |doi=10.1676/10-185.1 |issn=1559-4491|url-access=subscription }}</ref> It is closest [[Genetics|genetically]] to the [[Mindanao scops owl]] (''O. mirus)'' and the [[Mindoro scops owl]] (''O. mindorensis'') within the ''Otus'' genus, supported by their similar use of high altitude habitats. Besides their habitat preference, a genetic study also supports the evolutionary separation of the [[highland]] subgroup of Philippine-endemic scops owl species from those that prefer lowland habitats like the Philippine scops owl and its [[subspecies]] (''O. megalotis, O. everetti, O. nigrorum'') as well as the [[giant scops owl]] (''O. gurneyi'').<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" />

== Habitat and Distribution ==

The Luzon scops owl is known to be found in [[rainforest]], [[Montane ecosystems|montane forest]], and [[Luzon tropical pine forests|tropical pine forest]] habitats at altitudes generally ranging anywhere from 350 to 1,800 m, but it may also be found at heights upwards of 2,200m.<ref name=":2" /><ref name=":5">{{Cite journal |last=Poulsen |first=Michael Køie |date=1995 |title=The threatened and near-threatened birds of Luzon, Philippines, and the role of the Sierra Madre mountains in their conservation |url=https://www.cambridge.org/core/product/identifier/S0959270900002963/type/journal_article |journal=Bird Conservation International |language=en |volume=5 |issue=1 |pages=79–115 |doi=10.1017/S0959270900002963 |issn=0959-2709|doi-access=free }}</ref>

Although its habitat range across Luzon appears to be relatively large, it is noted to be uncommon and has a patchy distribution that corresponds to its preferred, highland habitats.<ref name=":6">{{Cite web |title=Luzon Highland Scops-owl (Otus longicornis) - BirdLife species factsheet |url=http://datazone.birdlife.org/species/factsheet/luzon-highland-scops-owl-otus-longicornis/distribution |access-date=2023-10-19 |website=datazone.birdlife.org}}</ref><ref name=":5" /> It has been recorded primarily in northern Luzon provinces such as [[Ilocos Norte]], [[Benguet]], [[Cagayan]], [[Isabela (province)|Isabela]], [[Nueva Ecija]], or near [[Manila]] in [[Bulacan]] and [[Quezon]]. However, they may be found as far south as provinces like [[Camarines Norte]] and [[Camarines Sur]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Oliver |first=William |date=2001 |title=Threatened Birds of the Philippines: The Haribon Foundation/Birdlife International Red Data Book |journal=Oryx |volume=35 |issue=1 |pages=91–92 |doi=10.1046/j.1365-3008.2001.0160b.x |issn=0030-6053}}</ref>

== Behaviour ==
According to limited sightings, the Luzon scops owl appears to be a [[Sedentary lifestyle|sedentary]] species, remaining in the same habitat year round.<ref name=":1" />  Like many owls, they have a nocturnal lifestyle.<ref name=":3" />

=== Vocalizations ===
Recorded male vocalizations appear to be more frequent at night and consist of a single note that lowers in pitch, each whistle being separated by 3 to 5 seconds of silence.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":5" /> Their vocalization is said to resemble that of the [[Eurasian scops owl]] (''Otus scops''), however the single whistle type of vocalization is characteristic of many other scops owl species.<ref name=":2" />

=== Diet ===
Little information is known about the species' diet. However, an inspection of the stomach contents of a few individuals revealed that they may be [[invertivore]]s, primarily feeding on insects.<ref name=":1" />

=== Reproduction ===

Information about reproduction is limited to a few sightings. Notably, chicks were observed to be born in May with light grey [[down feather]]s. The owls observed nested in a tree hole with a [[Clutch (eggs)|clutch]] size of 2 to 3 eggs per nest.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":7">{{Cite web |date=2016-10-01 |title=Luzon Highland Scops-owl: BirdLife International |url=https://www.iucnredlist.org/species/22688602/93202286 |access-date=2023-10-19 |website=IUCN Red List of Threatened Species}}</ref> Adult Luzon scops owls are thought to begin breeding at one year old with one clutch being produced per year.<ref name=":4" />

== Conservation ==

The [[IUCN Red List]] previously assessed the Luzon scops owl to be a near threatened species but in 2023, this was revised to [[Least-concern species]]. This downlisting does not mean the populations have been increasing but reflects new data that found this species to be much more common than previously thought. There is no known information on population size and little known on population trends besides the high likelihood that it is decreasing.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":7" /> This species is still declining due to habitat loss. Activities that contribute to [[deforestation]] such as logging, mining and the growth of agricultural land in the region of Luzon pose a threat to its endemic species.<ref name=":6" /><ref name=":7" /><ref name=":5" />

It is heavily suggested that critical habitats for the Luzon scops owl and other endangered or threatened birds be protected at all altitudes. Certain important areas [[Mount Pulag]] National Park are already protecting highland-dependant species and the [[Government of the Philippines|Philippine government]] is working on creating more protected areas through the [[List of protected areas of the Philippines|National Integrated Protected Area System]] (NIPAS).<ref name=":5" />

This species is found in protected areas including [[Mount Pulag|Mount Pulag Protected Park]] and [[Mounts Banahaw–San Cristobal Protected Landscape]] but actual protection from habitat loss and hunting are lax. 

Population surveys and frequent updates on the rates and effects of habitat loss within its range are strongly recommended to give a more accurate assessment of this species' conservation status.<ref name=":6" />
